title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS)
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Endocrinology Fellows, Critical Care, Emergency Medicine
objectives: Define HHS and distinguish from DKA
Understand insulin timing and dosing in HHS
Monitor osmolality and prevent complications (cerebral edema, overcorrection)

guidelineReferences.name: 2024 ADA/EASD Consensus Report: Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults
guidelineReferences.citation: Umpierrez GE, et al. Diabetes Care. 2024;47(8):1257-1275
keyPoints: HHS: Glucose >600 mg/dL, Osmolality ≥320 mOsm/kg, No significant ketosis
Lower insulin dose than DKA (0.05 units/kg/hr)
Osmolality drop ≤3-8 mOsm/kg/hr to prevent cerebral edema

vignette.text:
An 78-year-old man with Type 2 diabetes is brought from a nursing home with progressive lethargy and confusion over 5 days. Staff report he has had decreased oral intake due to a 'cold' and increased urination. He was last seen by a physician 3 months ago.

Past Medical History: Type 2 diabetes (on metformin and glipizide), Hypertension, Mild dementia, Prior stroke

Medications: Metformin 1000 mg BID, Glipizide 10 mg daily, Lisinopril 20 mg daily

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.HEENT: Extremely dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, weak pulses, delayed capillary refill
physicalExam.neuro: Obtunded, responds to painful stimuli only, no focal deficits
physicalExam.skin: Tenting, warm, no fruity odor on breath

other.Urinalysis: Glucose 4+, Ketones trace, WBC 50/hpf, bacteria present

questions.stem: This patient has glucose 892 mg/dL, osmolality 358 mOsm/kg, minimal ketosis.
questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for HHS?

options.text: HHS criteria (2024 Consensus): Glucose ≥600 mg/dL (or history of diabetes); Effective osmolality ≥320 mOsm/kg [2×Na + glucose/18]; NO significant ketoacidosis: Beta-hydroxybutyrate <3 mmol/L, pH >7.3, Bicarbonate ≥15 mEq/L; Severe dehydration and altered mental status common; Can have MIXED DKA/HHS if ketosis + hyperosmolality present
Any blood glucose >300 mg/dL
Ketoacidosis must be present
Normal mental status is required

questions.explanation: HHS Diagnostic Criteria (2024 ADA/EASD Consensus): GLUCOSE: ≥600 mg/dL (≥33.3 mmol/L) or history of diabetes; HYPEROSMOLALITY: Effective osmolality ≥320 mOsm/kg [Formula: 2×Na + glucose(mg/dL)/18]; NO SIGNIFICANT KETOACIDOSIS: Beta-hydroxybutyrate <3 mmol/L, pH >7.3, Bicarbonate ≥15 mEq/L. This patient meets criteria: Glucose 892, Effective osmolality 332, Beta-HB 1.2 (not significant ketosis), pH 7.32. Note: ~30% have MIXED DKA/HHS - treat as DKA if significant ketoacidosis present.

whyChain.why: Why is there no significant ketosis in HHS?
whyChain.answer: In HHS (typically Type 2 diabetes), there is enough residual insulin to suppress lipolysis and ketogenesis, but NOT enough to prevent hyperglycemia. In DKA (typically Type 1), complete insulin deficiency allows unrestrained lipolysis → ketone production. This is why HHS patients don't have fruity breath or Kussmaul breathing.

questions.stem: You need to distinguish HHS from DKA.
questions.question: What are the key differences between HHS and DKA?

options.text: HHS: Type 2 DM (usually), older patients, develops slowly (days-weeks), glucose often >600 mg/dL, marked hyperosmolality, minimal ketosis, high mortality (10-20%); DKA: Type 1 DM (usually), younger, develops quickly (hours), glucose 250-600 mg/dL, ketoacidosis (low pH, high AG), lower mortality (1-5%); Both can occur in any type of diabetes
HHS and DKA are identical conditions
DKA has higher mortality than HHS
Only Type 1 diabetics get hyperglycemic crises

questions.explanation: HHS vs DKA comparison: HHS: Type 2 DM typically, Older patients, Develops over days-weeks, Glucose often >600 (can be >1000) mg/dL, Severe hyperosmolality (≥320), Minimal/no ketosis, HIGH mortality (10-20% even with treatment), More severe dehydration (fluid deficit 8-12L). DKA: Type 1 DM typically (but can occur in T2), Younger patients, Develops over hours, Glucose 250-600 mg/dL (can be euglycemic), Ketoacidosis (pH <7.3, low bicarb, high AG), Lower mortality (1-5%), Fluid deficit 5-7L. OVERLAP: ~30% have mixed features - treat as DKA if ketoacidosis present.

questions.stem: This patient has a UTI and decreased oral intake.
questions.question: What are common precipitants of HHS?

options.text: INFECTION: Most common - UTI (this patient!), pneumonia, sepsis; POOR ACCESS TO WATER: Elderly, nursing home, dementia, stroke (impaired thirst or mobility); MEDICATION NON-ADHERENCE: Stopped diabetes meds; NEW MEDICATIONS: Steroids, thiazides, antipsychotics; ACUTE ILLNESS: MI, stroke, pancreatitis, surgery; NEW DIABETES: ~30% have no prior diagnosis
HHS has no identifiable precipitant
Only insulin omission causes HHS
Infection is rare as a precipitant

questions.explanation: HHS precipitants: (1) INFECTION: Most common (40-60%) - UTI (this patient has pyuria/bacteria), pneumonia, skin infections, sepsis; (2) POOR ACCESS TO FLUIDS: Key factor - elderly, dementia, nursing home residents, immobility, stroke with impaired thirst/swallowing; (3) MEDICATION: Non-adherence to diabetes meds, drugs that raise glucose (steroids, thiazides, beta-blockers, antipsychotics); (4) ACUTE ILLNESS: MI, stroke, pancreatitis, burns, surgery; (5) NEW DIABETES: 30% of HHS is first presentation of diabetes. This patient: UTI (infection) + nursing home/dementia (poor access to fluids) + respiratory infection ('cold').

questions.stem: This patient is severely dehydrated with BP 92/58.
questions.question: How should fluids be managed in HHS?

options.text: FLUIDS ARE MAINSTAY of HHS treatment (more important than insulin initially); Start: Isotonic saline (0.9% NS) or balanced crystalloid 500-1000 mL/hr for first 2-4 hours; Then: Switch to 0.45% NS if corrected sodium normal/high; Goal: Positive balance 3-6L in first 12h, remainder over next 12-24h; SLOWER correction than DKA - total deficit often 8-12L; Add dextrose when glucose <300 mg/dL
Limit fluids to prevent cerebral edema
Start with hypotonic fluids immediately
Insulin is more important than fluids

questions.explanation: Fluid resuscitation in HHS: FLUIDS ARE PRIMARY TREATMENT - often more important than insulin initially. Fluid deficit typically 8-12 L (100-220 mL/kg). PROTOCOL: (1) INITIAL: Isotonic saline (0.9% NS) or balanced crystalloid 500-1000 mL/hr for first 2-4 hours (smaller boluses 250 mL in elderly/heart failure); (2) REASSESS: After initial resuscitation, check corrected sodium; (3) SUBSEQUENT: If corrected Na normal/high → 0.45% NS (hypotonic), If corrected Na low → continue 0.9% NS; (4) GOAL: Positive balance 3-6 L in first 12 hours, remainder over next 12-24 hours; (5) ADD DEXTROSE: When glucose <300 mg/dL to prevent hypoglycemia and allow continued treatment.

whyChain.answer: HHS develops slowly (days-weeks) allowing brain adaptation to hyperosmolality. Rapid correction causes rapid fluid shifts into brain → cerebral edema. Target: Osmolality drop 3-8 mOsm/kg/hr, glucose drop 50-75 mg/dL/hr (not >90-120 mg/dL/hr). Young patients at highest risk for cerebral edema.

questions.stem: You've started IV fluids. When should insulin be started?
questions.question: How should insulin be managed in HHS?

options.text: DELAY insulin initially - fluids alone will lower glucose; Start insulin when glucose STOPS falling with fluids alone (often after 1-2 hours of resuscitation); LOWER dose than DKA: 0.05 units/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA); Goal: Glucose 250-300 mg/dL (not normal) until osmolality normalizes and patient alert; Add dextrose when glucose <300 mg/dL; Continue until mental status improves and osmolality normalized
Start insulin immediately at high doses
Never give insulin in HHS
Same insulin dose as DKA

questions.explanation: Insulin in HHS (different from DKA): (1) TIMING: DELAY insulin initially - IV fluids alone lower glucose significantly. Start insulin when glucose plateaus despite adequate fluids, typically 1-2 hours into resuscitation. Exception: Start immediately if significant ketosis (mixed DKA/HHS); (2) DOSE: LOWER than DKA - 0.05 units/kg/hr (vs 0.1 in DKA). Starting insulin too early/aggressively risks rapid osmotic shifts → cerebral edema; (3) GOAL: Maintain glucose 250-300 mg/dL until osmolality normalizes and mental status improves. Don't chase 'normal' glucose; (4) Add D5 to fluids when glucose <300 mg/dL; (5) TRANSITION: To SC insulin when eating and osmolality normalized.

whyChain.answer: HHS patients have residual insulin secretion (unlike DKA). Lower doses are sufficient. More importantly, aggressive insulin causes rapid glucose drop → rapid osmotic shifts → increased cerebral edema risk. The goal is SLOW, CONTROLLED correction. Fluids do most of the work in HHS.

questions.question: How should electrolytes be managed in HHS?

options.text: POTASSIUM: Total body K depleted despite normal/high serum K (similar to DKA); Start K replacement when K <5.3 mEq/L and urine output confirmed; Hold insulin if K <3.3 mEq/L until corrected; SODIUM: Measured Na often low (dilutional from glucose); Calculate corrected Na: Add 2.4 mEq/L per 100 mg/dL glucose >100; Monitor corrected Na - should rise slowly with treatment; PHOSPHATE/MAGNESIUM: Replace as needed
Potassium replacement not needed if serum K normal
Measured sodium reflects true sodium status
Aggressive potassium replacement even with high K

questions.explanation: Electrolyte management: POTASSIUM: Total body K depleted (urinary losses from osmotic diuresis) despite normal/elevated serum K. K will DROP with insulin and fluids. Replace when K <5.3 mEq/L and urine output adequate. HOLD insulin if K <3.3 mEq/L - risk of fatal arrhythmias. SODIUM: Measured Na is 'pseudohyponatremia' - dilutional from glucose drawing water into vascular space. Corrected Na = Measured Na + 2.4 × [(glucose-100)/100]. This patient: Measured 128 + 2.4 × 7.9 = 147 mEq/L (actually hypernatremic!). Monitor corrected Na - should decrease slowly (<10 mEq/L per 24h to prevent osmotic demyelination). PHOSPHATE/MAGNESIUM: Replace as needed.

questions.stem: You need to monitor this patient closely.
questions.question: What monitoring is required and what complications should be watched for?

options.text: MONITORING: Glucose q1-2h, Electrolytes/osmolality q4h, Clinical status/mental status frequently; TARGETS: Glucose drop 50-75 mg/dL/hr (max 90-120), Osmolality drop 3-8 mOsm/kg/hr, Na drop <10 mEq/L per 24h; COMPLICATIONS: Cerebral edema (young patients, rapid correction), VTE (hypercoagulable - prophylaxis essential), Vascular occlusion (MI, stroke), Rhabdomyolysis, AKI
Daily monitoring is sufficient
Rapid glucose normalization is the goal
VTE prophylaxis not needed

questions.explanation: Monitoring and complications: MONITORING: Glucose q1-2h, Electrolytes, BUN/Cr, osmolality q4h, Clinical assessment (mental status, I/O) frequently, Cardiac monitoring. RATE TARGETS (prevent complications): Glucose drop: 50-75 mg/dL/hr (max 90-120 mg/dL/hr), Osmolality drop: 3-8 mOsm/kg/hr, Na drop: <10 mEq/L per 24h. COMPLICATIONS: (1) Cerebral edema - especially in young patients, from rapid correction; (2) VTE - HHS is highly thrombogenic (dehydration, hyperviscosity), VTE prophylaxis ESSENTIAL; (3) Vascular events - MI, stroke, mesenteric ischemia; (4) Rhabdomyolysis; (5) AKI (prerenal, may persist); (6) ARDS.

questions.question: What is the complete management approach for this patient?

options.text: Immediate: ICU admission, 2 large-bore IVs, cardiac monitoring; (1) IV 0.9% NS 1L bolus then 500-1000 mL/hr for first 2-4h; (2) Hold insulin initially - start at 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose plateaus; (3) K replacement when K <5.3 and urine output confirmed; (4) Treat UTI with antibiotics; (5) VTE prophylaxis; (6) Monitor glucose q1-2h, lytes/osmolality q4h; (7) Goals: Slow correction - glucose drop <90-120/hr, osmolality drop 3-8/hr; (8) Add D5 when glucose <300; (9) Transition to SC insulin when eating and osmolality normal
Start insulin immediately at 0.1 units/kg/hr
Aggressive fluid restriction
Target normal glucose rapidly

questions.explanation: Complete HHS management: IMMEDIATE: ICU admission (high mortality), 2 large-bore IVs, cardiac monitoring, Foley catheter. TREATMENT: (1) FLUIDS FIRST: 0.9% NS 1 L bolus, then 500-1000 mL/hr (adjust for cardiac status). Reassess after 2-4 hours - switch to 0.45% NS if corrected Na normal/high; (2) INSULIN: DELAY initially - start 0.05 units/kg/hr when glucose stops falling with fluids alone (usually 1-2 hours); (3) POTASSIUM: Replace when K <5.3 mEq/L and urine output confirmed; (4) TREAT PRECIPITANT: Start antibiotics for UTI (this patient), look for other sources; (5) VTE PROPHYLAXIS: Essential - LMWH or heparin; (6) MONITORING: Glucose q1-2h, electrolytes/osmolality q4h, clinical assessment; (7) TARGETS: Glucose drop 50-75 mg/dL/hr (max 90-120), Osmolality drop 3-8 mOsm/kg/hr, Goal glucose 250-300 mg/dL until mental status improves; (8) Add D5 when glucose <300 mg/dL; (9) TRANSITION: SC insulin when eating, alert, and osmolality normalized.
